edges.quote: Rosamund Ball took a dagger in the belly that, three days later, proved to be the death of her
edges.note: The Jonquil's Pool attack's dagger wound directly killed Rosamund Ball three days later.
edges.rationale: Direct physical wound-to-death mechanism narrated with no intervening decision; real causation through a short delay, so CAUSES rather than TRIGGERS (the death was not the literal next beat, it followed a three-day interval) or MOTIVATES (no human decision mediates the wound producing death).

edges.quote: Forever after, she blamed her son's death on the women who attacked her at Maidenpool.
edges.note: Alysanne herself blamed the Maidenpool attackers for her son Aegon's death three days after birth; this is her own grief-driven claim, not Gyldayn's objective assertion. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break]
edges.rationale: The text explicitly frames this as Alysanne's personal belief ('she would say'), not the chronicler's own causal claim -- hedged, so tier-2 + disputed per Hard rule 5, routed as CAUSES on the in-universe claim rather than MOTIVATES since no further decision-event is the target.

edges.quote: But the young prince had come too early. Small and frail, he died three days after birth.
edges.note: Role edge grounding the newly minted death event to the infant prince, matching the PARTICIPATES_IN/VICTIM_IN pattern used for the other Jaehaerys-era births and deaths in this section.
edges.rationale: Role edge, not causal ladder; gives the new node its subject anchor.

edges.quote: Her Grace also held a women’s court at Gulltown, and a second at the Gates of the Moon;
edges.note: The Widow's Law stub (imposition-of-the-gate-fee's sibling) had zero causal edges; what Alysanne learned at the women's courts held during the king's Vale progress is what the text says directly produced the law. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break]
edges.rationale: A human decision (Jaehaerys's, on Alysanne's counsel) mediates the link between what she learned and the decree, so MOTIVATES rather than CAUSES (agency-collapse check); routed to the event-target form since promulgation-of-the-widows-law is a specific downstream decision-event.

edges.quote: the challenge of paying for the completion of the Dragonpit and the rebuilding of King’s Landing strained even his talents. The taxes on silk, spice, and crenellations alone could not answer, so Lord Rego reluctantly imposed a new levy: a gate fee
edges.note: Stub wiring: the ongoing cost of finishing the Dragonpit (plus the city rebuild) is what the text says forced Rego Draz to impose the gate fee.
edges.rationale: Rego Draz's own decision to levy the fee mediates the financial pressure, so MOTIVATES (agency-collapse check) rather than CAUSES; sourced from the still-open Dragonpit construction project since no separate 'city rebuilding' node exists.

edges.quote: On the isle of Driftmark, Daemon Velaryon's eldest son became a father for the
edges.note: Stub wiring: identifies Corwyn Velaryon (Daemon Velaryon's eldest son per the Corwyn/Daemon node pair) as the father in this birth event. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break]
edges.rationale: Role edge, not causal ladder; gives the birth-of-corlys-velaryon stub its first edge.

edges.quote: The babe was named Corlys, after the great-great-uncle who had served so nobly as the Lord Commander of the first Kingsguard
edges.note: Stub wiring: matches the PARTICIPATES_IN pattern already used for the sibling birth events in this section (Boremund, Daenerys, Aemon).
edges.rationale: Role edge; the newborn's own node is the correct target for PARTICIPATES_IN per the established convention in this section's other birth edges.

edges.quote: Corlys Velaryon, the boy born on Driftmark in 53 AC, would take his ship the Sea Snake on nine great voyages, sailing farther than any man of Westeros had ever sailed before.
edges.note: Cross-section causal-spine link: Corlys's birth (section 10) is the precondition for his adult voyaging career (section 13), including reaching Asshai.
edges.rationale: Birth is a precondition, not a force compelling the later voyages (Corlys's own choices as a grown man determined he would sail) -- ENABLES preserves that agency rather than a false CAUSES.

edges.quote: the shipwrights of Braavos had completed work on the carrack Sun Chaser, the dream Elissa Farman had purchased with her stolen dragon's eggs.
edges.note: The gold from selling the three stolen dragon eggs to the Sealord funded the Sun Chaser's construction, which is what let Elissa Farman/Alys Westhill later depart on her voyage.
edges.rationale: The theft funded the ship (a precondition) but her decision to build and sail it was her own free choice, so ENABLES rather than CAUSES.

edges.quote: the great fire that had swept across the Disputed Lands a fortnight past. Fanned by strong winds and fed by dry grasses, the blaze had raged for three days and three nights, engulfing half a dozen villages and one free company, the Adventurers, who found themselves trapped between the onrushing flames and a Tyroshi host under the command of the Archon himself.
edges.note: Stub wiring: no node previously existed for the wind-driven wildfire that trapped and destroyed the free company the Adventurers; minted great-fire-in-the-disputed-lands as the chain anchor.
edges.rationale: The fire's entrapment of the company between flames and a hostile army is a direct physical/military mechanism, not a mediating human decision by the victims, so CAUSES.

edges.quote: the magisters of Pentos were so unhappy with the terms that they sacrificed their prince to their queer gods, as is the custom of that city
edges.note: Stub wiring: the Pentoshi magisters' anger at the treaty terms is what the text says drove them to sacrifice their prince.
edges.rationale: The magisters' collective decision to sacrifice the prince mediates the link between the treaty and the sacrifice, so MOTIVATES (agency-collapse check) with the event as target rather than a false CAUSES; text states this as plain fact rather than a hedged rumor, so tier-1.

edges.quote: Lord Commander Burley also renamed Snowgate castle in her
edges.note: Stub wiring: Lord Commander Burley's renaming of Snowgate was an honor prompted by Alysanne's visit and patronage of the Night's Watch during her Northern progress. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break]
edges.rationale: Burley's own decision to rename the castle mediates the link, so MOTIVATES rather than CAUSES; routed to the event-target form.

edges.quote: The answer she proposed was a New Gift, a further strip of land south of Brandon's Gift.
edges.note: What Alysanne saw of the Night's Watch's poverty during her progress to the North is what the text says produced her proposal for the New Gift.
edges.rationale: Alaric Stark and the queen's own decisions mediate the doubling of the Gift, so MOTIVATES; both this and D-E12/D-E14 fan out from the same progress node, matching the roster's zero-causal-edge gap on queen-alysannes-progress-to-the-north.

edges.quote: Alysanne had anticipated that objection. She would pay for the castle herself,
edges.note: Alysanne's visit to Castle Black during her Northern progress is what led her to pledge her own jewels to fund the new castle, Deep Lake. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break]
edges.rationale: Her own decision to pay mediates the link, so MOTIVATES rather than CAUSES.

edges.quote: she held her women’s court amongst the whores and strumpets of Mole’s Town...and there heard
edges.note: Marquee chain the text narrates at length: what Alysanne heard from the women of Mole's Town (the blacksmith's daughter's story above all) is what she brought to the small council, leading directly to the abolition of the first night. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break]
edges.rationale: Jaehaerys's council decision mediates the link between the testimony and the law, so MOTIVATES with the decision-event as target rather than a false CAUSES.

edges.quote: As their larders and granaries emptied, famine spread across the land, and old men bade farewell to their children and went out into the snow to die so their kin might live.
edges.note: Seed row 139: wires the winter-of-59-60-ac stub (previously zero edges despite bundling this whole chain into its own Identity prose) forward into a new famine-of-59-60-ac node, which also carries the hoarding/bread-price-rise consequence the text narrates in the same passage.
edges.rationale: Direct plain Gyldayn narration of cause and effect (failed crops/emptied larders producing famine); no human decision mediates the link, so CAUSES. Did not further split the famine into a separate 'bread prices' node -- that consequence is a market-level effect of the same famine, not a distinct narrated event, and is captured in the new node's identity to avoid over-minting.

edges.quote: One struck the Autumn Moon and split her mast from the crow's nest down to the deck.
edges.note: Stub wiring: minted storms-scatter-the-sun-chasers-fleet as the chain anchor for the three storms Eustace Hightower describes, since no node previously existed for them even though they produced two roster/stub events (this one and the islands' discovery).
edges.rationale: A lightning strike directly destroying the ship's mast, leading to her sinking, is a physical mechanism with no mediating decision, so CAUSES.

edges.quote: The winds and raging seas that had separated her Sun Chaser from the Hightowers had driven her westward, and when the dawn broke, her man in the crow's nest had espied birds circling a hazy mountain peak on the horizon.
edges.note: The same storms that sank the Autumn Moon also physically drove the Sun Chaser west onto the three uninhabited islands.
edges.rationale: Wind/current physically driving the ship, with the crew then simply sighting land, is a direct physical mechanism, not a human decision, so CAUSES.

edges.quote: So the voyagers had parted ways as they left the islands. Lady Meredith turned back east for home, whilst Alys Westhill and her Sun Chaser pressed on westward, chasing the sun.
edges.note: The islands let both battered ships resupply and repair; from there Alys Westhill chose to sail on alone into the unknown, never to be seen again.
edges.rationale: The islands were a precondition (repair/resupply stop) that made the onward voyage possible, but Alys Westhill's decision to press on west (over Eustace Hightower's refusal) is a free choice, so ENABLES rather than CAUSES (agency-collapse check).

edges.quote: There he lost his love and half his crew, if the tales be true...and there as well, in Asshai’s harbor, he glimpsed an old and much weathered ship
edges.note: Reaching Asshai's harbor is the immediate occasion of Corlys's sighting of the ship he believed was the Sun Chaser -- same beat, no intervening steps. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break] [S204 adjudication: TRIGGERS->ENABLES — arrival is precondition for the sighting, not a spark]
edges.rationale: Arriving in the harbor and glimpsing the ship are effectively the same narrative moment with nothing decisional between them, so TRIGGERS; kept at tier-2/disputed matching the existing sun-chaser-glimpsed-in-asshais-harbor node's own hedge ('if the tales be true...he would swear').

new_nodes.name: Death of Prince Aegon (son of Jaehaerys I)
new_nodes.identity: Alysanne's son Aegon, named for the Conqueror and her lost brother, was born too early and died three days after birth; Alysanne forever blamed the Maidenpool attackers.

new_nodes.name: Great fire in the Disputed Lands
new_nodes.identity: A wind-driven wildfire raged for three days and nights across the Disputed Lands, engulfing half a dozen villages and trapping the free company the Adventurers between the flames and a Tyroshi host.

new_nodes.name: Famine of 59-60 AC
new_nodes.identity: Failed harvests across the North, riverlands, westerlands, Vale, and Reach following the cruel winter of 59-60 AC; larders and granaries emptied, some old folk went out into the snow to die so their kin might live, and hoarding drove up the price of bread across the Seven Kingdoms.

new_nodes.name: Storms scatter the Sun Chaser's fleet
new_nodes.identity: Three storms battered the Sun Chaser, Lady Meredith, and Autumn Moon far out in the Sunset Sea; lightning split the Autumn Moon, which went down with all hands, while the Sun Chaser was driven west onto three uncharted islands.

flags: founder-of-the-autumn-moon: the node name is grammatically ambiguous ('Founder' reads as a person-noun, e.g. 'the founder of the Autumn Moon,' rather than the verb 'founder' = to sink). The underlying content is a real, correctly-typed event (event.shipwreck, 'ship foundered; Eustace's brother and all his men drowned', matching fab-the-long-reign-13:43 'I saw the Autumn Moon founder with my own eyes'), so it was wired (D-E17) rather than left unwired -- but a rename to 'Foundering of the Autumn Moon' would remove the ambiguity.

archon-of-tyrosh-war-of-the-ninepenny-kings (section 12 roster item) is typed event.war but its existing edges (HOLDS_TITLE, NEGOTIATES_WITH, CULTURE_OF, BETRAYS) show it's actually being used as a character/person node (the Archon himself), not an event. Out of scope for this assignment but flagged for a schema-drift pass.

death-of-prince-aegon-son-of-jaehaerys-i is a new mint not listed in the packet's stubs, but the attack->death chain the text asserts (Alysanne's own claim) was too load-bearing to leave ungrounded; kept tier-2/disputed given the in-universe framing.

The winter-of-59-60-ac stub already carried its whole downstream chain (crops die -> famine -> bread prices) baked into one node's Identity prose with zero graph edges; minted famine-of-59-60-ac as a single consolidated downstream node per the seed rather than further splitting 'bread prices rise' into its own node, to avoid over-minting a single short paragraph.

harvest.note: Lord Manderly roasts an entire aurochs and stages a small tourney to welcome Queen Alysanne at White Harbor; his daughter Jessamyn serves as her cupbearer with strong northern ale.
Alysanne makes Rego Draz and Jaehaerys each drink a tankard of King's Landing's foul river water at council to win approval for the well-and-pipe project later known as 'the queen's fountains.'
Jaehaerys at 24: handsome golden beard shot through with silver, unshorn hair in a thick braid to his waist, tall, graceful, warm smile / cold frown.

Septon Barth's privy account of what came out of Aerea Targaryen's body after her ride to Valyria on Balerion -- 'worms with faces...snakes with hands' -- vivid deep-lore/Doom-adjacent material not yet attached to any node.
Sothoryos fever/parasite horror suffered by Eustace Hightower's crew -- 'his skin was sloughing off, and he was bleeding from his ears and cock and arse' -- vivid disease description.
